title: Stage Gates and Required Readiness
description: Understand the readiness criteria and gate requirements before moving opportunities to the next stage.

sections.title: Stage Gates and Required Readiness
sections.content:
Stage gates are the guardrails of your sales process. They prevent premature deal advancement, ensure critical information is captured, and maintain forecast accuracy. Most importantly: you move a deal forward when the gate criteria are met, not because time has passed or you want to.

Gate: Lead to Qualification

To create an Opportunity, these must be ready:

- Lead qualified and converted into account hierarchy
- Parent and Entity Child Accounts established
- Primary contact identified and confirmed
- Initial customer need understood (at least one conversation)

Gate: Qualification to Solution Definition

Before moving to discovery and solution design:

- Named Primary Contact: The specific person you'll work with throughout the sales cycle
- Next Steps Locked In: Specific action documented ("Demo on March 15," "Technical discovery April 2")
- Scope Understood: What problem are they solving? How many users? What modules? What's the rough size?
- Timeline Confirmed: When does the customer expect to decide?

Gate: Solution Definition to Pricing & Quotation

Before you quote, you need clarity:

- Products Identified: You know exactly what you'll propose
- Pricing Model Documented: Per-user? Per-transaction? Flat fee? Consumption-based? Be explicit.
- Scope Locked: Users, modules, service level, geography—locked down enough to quote
- Internal Alignment: Sales, account management, and technical specialists agree on the solution
- Customer Expectation Set: They understand the direction and are ready for a proposal

Gate: Pricing & Quotation to Customer Review

Before sending a quote:

- Quote prepared with full details (products, quantities, pricing, SLAs, terms, special conditions)
- Quote approved by pricing authority and appropriate gatekeepers
- Customer briefing scheduled (never send quotes cold)

Gate: Customer Review to Contracting

Before engaging legal:

- Customer accepted pricing, scope, and terms (or accepted your revisions)
- All customer questions answered to satisfaction
- Contract signatory identified and confirmed as authorized
- Legal/contracting team engaged and ready to draft

Gate: Contracting to Closed Won

Before you close:

- Contract executed by authorized signatories from both sides (or written acceptance obtained)
- All commercial terms finalized
- Payment terms and invoicing confirmed
- Win reason documented for analysis

Managing Exceptions

Exceptions happen. If you need to bypass a gate, get documented approval from your manager or a gatekeeper. Record the exception in the opportunity and capture the missing information immediately afterward.

Why Gates Matter

Gates do more than enforce discipline:

- Forecast Accuracy: Leadership trusts the numbers because gates ensure real progress
- Customer Success: Customers get engaged at the right time with the right information
- Problem Detection: Issues surface early, not in the final stretch
- Shared Understanding: The whole organization knows what each stage means
- Efficient Deals: Systematic progression means faster, cleaner closes

Respect the gates. They protect your business and improve your win rate.

keyTakeaways: Gate requirements must be satisfied before advancing to the next stage; do not move forward just because time has passed
Gates prevent premature advancement, ensure critical information is captured, and maintain data quality and forecast accuracy
Exceptions require documented approval from a manager or gatekeeper
